step_by_step_reasoning: We start with incoming real-time cloud provider events (UserCompanyDataStream) sent to the Kinesis Data Stream for Collection. The data is processed and stored in Amazon Neptune as the primary graph database. Changes are synchronized to Elasticsearch (OpenSearch) and DynamoDB (Backing Store). A Sharder Service monitors metrics in CloudWatch from Neptune and Elasticsearch to balance write operations. For rules evaluation, resource updates flow from Neptune to a second Kinesis Data Stream (Rules Engine). The Rules Engine queries Neptune, writes security violations to a second DynamoDB instance, and dispatches alerts via SES (Email) and ThirdParty channels (Slack/Splunk).

graph.name: CloudHealth SecureState Real-Time Security Monitoring Architecture
graph.notes: Handles billions of events daily to secure 50M+ cloud assets. Uses Amazon Neptune for graph-based resource modeling, supplemented by Elasticsearch and DynamoDB. Employs a custom Sharder service to dynamically load balance write streams based on real-time CloudWatch metrics.

nodes.name: Cloud Provider Events
nodes.notes: Real-time event logs from AWS, Azure, and GCP.

nodes.name: Kinesis (Collection)
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Collects billions of resource state events daily.

nodes.name: Amazon Neptune
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Graph DB holding 50M+ interconnected resources.

nodes.name: Elasticsearch
nodes.notes: Powers searching, analytical aggregations, and reporting.

nodes.name: Kinesis (Rules Engine)
nodes.notes: Triggers rules execution on resource changes.

nodes.name: Slack & Splunk Alerts
nodes.notes: Routes real-time alerts to third-party tools.

edges.notes: Queries Neptune graph to check rules.
